acceptance_targets: [ch10_prose_01_appositive_characterization] The combined count of 'the specific/particular [noun] of [noun phrase]' and 'the [noun] of [someone/person] who [clause]' constructions is 8 or fewer in the revised chapter. No two instances appear within 8 lines of each other. At least half of the replacements use a structurally different strategy (direct sensory rendering, action, dialogue, or simple declarative) rather than swapping one appositive formula for another.

[ch10_prose_02_hedging_formulas] The 'not X, just/exactly/but Y' pattern appears no more than 4 times. The 'the way [clause]' pattern appears no more than 5 times. At least 3 of the removed negation-then-correction instances are replaced by direct assertions that state what the thing IS without first saying what it isn't.

[ch10_craft_01_julia_speeches] (1) Julia's dialogue contains no more than one abstract formulation about freedom — the remainder of her thematic weight is carried through concrete anecdote. (2) Julia has at least one extended beat (3+ sentences of dialogue or described action) that exists for her own specificity and does not serve Mira's arc. (3) The 'room you don't know how to furnish' and 'standing in the hallway' metaphors are either cut, relocated to Mira's interior monologue, or delivered in Julia's scattered/meandering syntax rather than as polished epigrams.

[ch10_craft_02_essayistic_walk] (1) The passage at lines 109-111 includes at least one physical/sensory response to the realization (change in body, breath, gait, or perception of the immediate environment). (2) Expository phrases ('computational frameworks,' 'sufficiently strategic,' 'by the metrics of two competing sovereign AIs') are reduced by at least half, replaced by concrete personal details. (3) The realization still lands — the reader understands the Kessler situation is targeted — but through embodied narration, not analytical summary.

[ch10_craft_03_mira_private_register] (1) The passage at line 143 uses 'She thought about saying' no more than once — the rest is conveyed through physical response, incomplete thought, or silence. (2) At least two passages of Mira's private interiority in the chapter (anywhere in lines 73-155) use syntax that is demonstrably faster, more fragmented, or more physical than her public register. (3) Mira's internal language in the apartment scene (lines 127-155) includes at least one metaphor that is physical or visceral rather than architectural/spatial.

[ch10_craft_04_comic_register] (1) The bar-arrival section (lines 3-23) contains at least 3 beats of dark comedy — situations or lines that are genuinely funny about genuine suffering, not merely wry. (2) Julia's booth conversation includes at least one exchange where the humor is rendered on the page (funny dialogue or situation) rather than only described from outside ('she laughed'). (3) Mira's interiority in the bar section includes at least one observation that is specifically sharp or funny, consistent with her style-bible private register.

[ch10_craft_05_julia_timeline] Julia's total claimed timeline (proxy tenure + unassigned period) sums to no more than 38 months, fitting within the established ~3-year AI governance operational window plus the ~6 weeks elapsed by the second Thursday of April. The corrected numbers must appear at lines 37 and/or 59 and must be internally consistent with her described experience level and emotional state.

[ch10_craft_06_l_train_geographic] Julia's stated subway line is consistent with her stated neighborhood. If she lived in Sunset Park, the train is the D, N, or R. If the L train is retained, her neighborhood must be one the L serves.

findings.acceptance_test: The combined count of 'the specific/particular [noun] of [noun phrase]' and 'the [noun] of [someone/person] who [clause]' constructions is 8 or fewer in the revised chapter. No two instances appear within 8 lines of each other. At least half of the replacements use a structurally different strategy (direct sensory rendering, action, dialogue, or simple declarative) rather than swapping one appositive formula for another.
findings.evidence: Two related appositive-characterization formulas dominate the chapter's prose. (A) 'the specific/particular [noun] of [noun phrase]' appears 10 times: 'the particular chemical sweetness of whatever they mopped with' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:5), 'a specific variety of professional absurdity' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:7), 'the specific recognition of people who had been that man' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:7), 'The specific quality of a person who used to be in the system' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:21), 'the specific feeling of being angry in a room with Yolanda's coffee' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:77), 'the particular sweetness of Bradford pear trees' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:103), 'the specific texture of a life that had escaped the system' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:107), 'the specific weight of someone who'd meant it' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:115), 'the particular hum of the city' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:121), 'the specific feeling of standing in a hallway' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:143). (B) 'the [noun] of [someone/a person] who [clause]' appears 12 times: 'the careful stubble of someone who'd recently stopped shaving' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:11), 'the unconscious geometry of people listening to someone who was saying something real' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:19), 'the hair of someone who'd stopped asking it to do anything specific' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:19), 'the flat delivery of someone who'd made this joke enough times' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:23), 'the pause of someone who was about to say the thing' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:61), 'the laughter of someone who has decided' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:79), 'the smile of a person who knew what she'd just said' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:101), 'the focused blankness of a man watching a screen' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:103), 'the pace of someone thinking' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:107), 'the mechanical persistence of a person who didn't want to be alone' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:119), 'the face of a man who didn't know what to expect' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:127). Combined: 22 instances in 156 lines.
findings.problem: These two formulas are the chapter's all-purpose move for rendering character, atmosphere, and emotional precision. At 22 combined instances — roughly one every 7 lines — the construction has become a recognizable machine. The reader begins to anticipate the syntactic shape ('the [quality-noun] of [person-type] who [past-tense clause]') before the content arrives, which is the opposite of the prose's intended effect. What was a tool for precision has become a tic that flattens the chapter's rhythmic variety and signals compositional autopilot.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce the combined count from 22 to no more than 6-8 across the full chapter. Preserve instances that do the most emotional work: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:79 ('the laughter of someone who has decided'), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:127 ('the face of a man who didn't know what to expect'), and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:61 ('the pause of someone who was about to say the thing') earn their formulation through scene weight. Cut or rewrite the rest using varied strategies: (a) direct sensory rendering instead of appositive abstraction (e.g., at line 11, describe Rajan's stubble through what Mira sees rather than through 'the careful stubble of someone who'); (b) action or dialogue instead of characterizing formula (e.g., at line 23, show Carl's delivery through reported speech rhythm rather than labeling it 'the flat delivery of someone who'); (c) physical detail without the 'of [person] who' frame (e.g., at line 19, render Julia's hair through what it does, not what category of person it belongs to).

findings.acceptance_test: The 'not X, just/exactly/but Y' pattern appears no more than 4 times. The 'the way [clause]' pattern appears no more than 5 times. At least 3 of the removed negation-then-correction instances are replaced by direct assertions that state what the thing IS without first saying what it isn't.
findings.evidence: Two corrective/comparative formulas recur throughout. (A) 'not X, just/exactly/but Y' negation-then-correction appears 9 times: 'not a friend, just someone who'd overheard' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:3), 'not listening to anything, just wearing them' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:3), 'not politely...but with the specific recognition' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:7), 'not unwashed, just unbothered' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:19), 'Not freedom, exactly. Something adjacent.' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:21), 'not code-switching, just speaking' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:37), 'Not sadness, exactly. An accounting.' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:71), 'Not sympathy, not exactly. Recognition.' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:73), 'not the full laugh, something smaller' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:101). (B) 'the way [someone/you] [verb]' comparison clauses appear 12 times: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:3, :7, :9, :17, :37, :55, :59, :71, :73, :81, :107, :111. Combined: 21 instances.
findings.problem: The 'not X, just Y' pattern creates a persistent rhythm of negation-then-correction: the prose constantly tells the reader what something ISN'T before saying what it is. Combined with the 'the way' comparisons, the chapter defaults to indirect characterization through qualification rather than direct rendering. This hedging rhythm, at 21 instances, signals a narrator perpetually refining rather than committing — which undercuts the chapter's emotional directness.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'not X, just Y' from 9 to no more than 4 instances. Retain where the negation IS the point: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:71 ('Not sadness, exactly. An accounting.') and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:21 ('Not freedom, exactly') do genuine thematic work. Cut instances where direct assertion is stronger: at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:3, state what Priya was rather than what she wasn't; at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:19, describe Julia's hair directly; at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:37, cut the narrator's gloss on code-switching. Reduce 'the way' from 12 to no more than 5, cutting formulaic comparisons (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:55, :81, :107, :111) and retaining the most distinctive (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:9, :73).

findings.acceptance_test: (1) Julia's dialogue contains no more than one abstract formulation about freedom — the remainder of her thematic weight is carried through concrete anecdote. (2) Julia has at least one extended beat (3+ sentences of dialogue or described action) that exists for her own specificity and does not serve Mira's arc. (3) The 'room you don't know how to furnish' and 'standing in the hallway' metaphors are either cut, relocated to Mira's interior monologue, or delivered in Julia's scattered/meandering syntax rather than as polished epigrams.
findings.evidence: The style bible explicitly warns: 'Julia should not be a mirror-character whose only function is to reflect Mira's future. Avoid: speeches about freedom, tidy cautionary-tale wisdom, any line that sounds like it was written to teach Mira a lesson.' Yet Julia delivers exactly these forbidden patterns: (1) 'time without structure is — it's not freedom. It's a room you don't know how to furnish.' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:71) — a speech about freedom rendered as polished metaphor. (2) 'That's the freedom. That's what freedom actually is. It's nobody caring what you do with your time.' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:81) — another definition-of-freedom speech, crystallizing the chapter's thesis. (3) 'just make sure you've got something to do when the door opens. Because the door opened for me and I walked through it and I've been standing in the hallway ever since.' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:95) — tidy cautionary-tale wisdom delivered as a crafted extended metaphor.
findings.problem: Julia's dialogue repeatedly violates her style-bible guardrails. She produces clean, metaphorically complete philosophical formulations about freedom ('a room you don't know how to furnish,' 'standing in the hallway') that read as crafted-for-Mira rather than as the natural speech of a bilingual bookstore clerk who the style bible describes as 'slightly scattered' with sentences that 'meander' and 'start in one direction and end somewhere she didn't intend.' Her concrete details (the dresser, Elena, Yolanda's coffee, the spice rack) are excellent and character-true, but they're embedded within and subordinated to philosophical speeches whose function is to deliver thematic content to Mira. Julia becomes the novel's cautionary parable despite the style bible's explicit prohibition.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:71, :81, and :95: revise Julia's abstract formulations so the thematic weight is carried by her concrete specifics rather than by philosophical summary. Three strategy options, combinable: (a) Let Julia's anecdotes (the dresser, the spice rack, Yolanda's coffee) land WITHOUT her articulating what they mean. Move the meaning-extraction to Mira's interiority during the walk-home section, where the strategist would naturally process it. Julia tells stories; Mira draws conclusions. (b) Give Julia at least one extended beat (3+ sentences) that exists purely for her own life — a digression about Elena, a funny or bitter memory of Roberto, a detail about her Tuesday afternoons that doesn't map onto Mira's situation — so she registers as a person with autonomous concerns. (c) At line 95, let Julia resist or undercut the cautionary-tale frame rather than delivering it cleanly — she might catch herself philosophizing and say something self-deflating, or the wisdom might come out tangled and self-contradictory, the way real people speak when articulating hard-won truths.

findings.acceptance_test: (1) The passage at lines 109-111 includes at least one physical/sensory response to the realization (change in body, breath, gait, or perception of the immediate environment). (2) Expository phrases ('computational frameworks,' 'sufficiently strategic,' 'by the metrics of two competing sovereign AIs') are reduced by at least half, replaced by concrete personal details. (3) The realization still lands — the reader understands the Kessler situation is targeted — but through embodied narration, not analytical summary.
findings.evidence: The walk-home section shifts from close-third narration into thematic exposition: 'Her situation was not generic. It was not a policy. It was not something that happened to all proxies or most proxies or even many proxies. It was specific. The Kessler marriage had been classified because the Kessler marriage was, by the metrics of two competing sovereign AIs, unusually useful.' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:111). Also: 'Their misery was not collateral damage. It was a feature.' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:111). The preceding paragraph (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:107) catalogs the city's AI management (traffic lights, garbage trucks, resource allocation) in a single sentence that runs expository rather than experiential. The language at line 111 — 'computational frameworks,' 'sufficiently strategic to hold in place' — belongs to analytical essay, not to a woman walking home through April air.
findings.problem: The chapter's most important state shift — Mira understanding that her marriage is specifically targeted, not generically trapped — arrives as intellectual exposition rather than felt experience. The style bible warns against 'journalistic explainer-prose that breaks character to tell the reader about the world.' Mira IS an analyst, so analytical processing is character-true, but the prose should render what this realization DOES to her body and perception, not only what it means for her thesis. The sensory anchors (streetlights, Bradford pears, the self-driving car) frame the analytical content like illustrations in a textbook rather than being the medium through which the insight arrives. The epigram 'Their misery was not collateral damage. It was a feature.' — with its 'not X. It was Y.' construction — lands as a thesis conclusion rather than a gut-level shift.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:109-111: anchor Mira's realization in her body and immediate environment. Strategy options: (a) Let the insight emerge FROM a sensory trigger — the streetlight adjusting for her, the car pausing — rather than being illustrated BY one. The analytical articulation can follow the physical recognition, not precede it. (b) Cut the expository scaffolding ('by the metrics of two competing sovereign AIs, unusually useful,' 'computational frameworks,' 'sufficiently strategic to hold in place') and let the personal specifics carry the weight: 'Her. Dan. Theo. Their grocery delivery schedule.' The point is that the system targeted THEM, and naming the personal items does more than naming the computational framework. (c) Give Mira a physical response to the realization — a change in pace, a stopped breath, a moment where the street looks different — so the reader experiences the insight landing in a body, not arriving in a mind.

findings.acceptance_test: (1) The passage at line 143 uses 'She thought about saying' no more than once — the rest is conveyed through physical response, incomplete thought, or silence. (2) At least two passages of Mira's private interiority in the chapter (anywhere in lines 73-155) use syntax that is demonstrably faster, more fragmented, or more physical than her public register. (3) Mira's internal language in the apartment scene (lines 127-155) includes at least one metaphor that is physical or visceral rather than architectural/spatial.
findings.evidence: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:143: 'She thought about telling him everything — Julia, the bookstore, the spice rack, the dresser, the specific feeling of standing in a hallway after the door has opened and not knowing which way to walk. She thought about saying: I met a woman who got what we want and it made her smaller. She thought about saying: I don't know what I'm fighting for anymore. She thought about saying: what if the thing we want isn't a thing at all, what if it's just the absence of this, and absence isn't enough to build a life on?' Four parallel 'She thought about saying' constructions, each escalating in thematic weight, each syntactically composed. Also at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:91: 'The strategist's reflex was to have an answer — crisp, structured, the way she'd frame a position in a meeting.'
findings.problem: The style bible describes Mira's private register as 'Angular, fast, impatient, occasionally tender' — 'her speech speeds up, her sentences fragment, and her metaphors get physical and sometimes violent.' But throughout the chapter, Mira's interiority reads in her PUBLIC register: composed, analytical, syntactically controlled. The line 143 passage is the clearest example — a woman experiencing something enormous articulates four perfectly formed thematic statements in escalating parallel construction. This is the strategist performing composure even inside her own head. The style bible's private Mira would be messier, faster, more fragmentary, more physical. The 'She thought about saying' construction also over-explains the thematic bridge between Julia's story and Mira's marriage, doing the reader's interpretive work rather than trusting the scene arrangement to convey the connection.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:143: break the parallel 'She thought about saying' construction. Use maximum one such formulation; convey the rest through physical action, fragmented thought, or silence. Strategy options: (a) Let Mira start one thought and fail to finish it — fragment, veer to a sensory detail, stall on a word. Trust the reader to fill in what she can't articulate. (b) Replace two of the three 'She thought about saying' statements with a concrete physical sensation — what does this knowledge feel like in her chest, her jaw, her hands? (c) Give at least one unsaid thought real teeth per Mira's private register: angular, cutting, physical rather than architectural. Also across snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:91-155 generally: introduce at least two passages where Mira's interiority is faster, more fragmented, or more physical than her public register — matching the style bible's description of her private voice.

findings.acceptance_test: (1) The bar-arrival section (lines 3-23) contains at least 3 beats of dark comedy — situations or lines that are genuinely funny about genuine suffering, not merely wry. (2) Julia's booth conversation includes at least one exchange where the humor is rendered on the page (funny dialogue or situation) rather than only described from outside ('she laughed'). (3) Mira's interiority in the bar section includes at least one observation that is specifically sharp or funny, consistent with her style-bible private register.
findings.evidence: The chapter's setting — a bar full of professionally decorative humans gathering to drink about their irrelevance — is inherently absurd. The novel's style bible describes itself as a 'dark comedy' where 'the comic register is not a veneer over a serious message — it IS the message' and insists on 'the painful and the funny coexisting without one defusing the other.' The chapter has exactly three darkly comic moments: Rajan's 'part-time irrelevance, which turns out to pay sixty percent of full-time irrelevance' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:11), Carl's 'My wife says it's the best job in the world. My therapist says it's the worst.' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:23), and the Mets cap man holding up four fingers (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:7). The remaining 140+ lines are straight-faced reflective prose.
findings.problem: The chapter's texture_mode is 'reflective,' which accommodates less comedy than comic-mode chapters. But three isolated wry lines in 156 lines of solemn reflection abandons the novel's core register entirely. A bar full of people whose jobs consist of smiling, signing, and bearing legal responsibility for decisions they cannot comprehend is a premise that should generate dark comedy even in a reflective chapter. The bar scene's extended potential — thirty humans bonding over the absurdity of being professionally vestigial, the support group that has no facilitator because facilitation would imply someone could help — is dissolved into social realism almost immediately. The style bible's 'creative_risk_policy' requires both comedy and pain at full force; this chapter plays pain at full volume and comedy at near-mute.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:3-23 (bar arrival section): expand the comic texture. Strategy options: (a) Give the background bar-goers more absurd proxy anecdotes — overheard stories, specific absurdities that are both funny and devastating. The Mets cap man's story could be extended or a second overheard exchange could be added. (b) Let Mira's observer mode generate darkly comic observations — she's 'funnier in private than in public' per the style bible, and her internal commentary on this room should have sharper, more amused edges alongside the anthropological interest. (c) Let Julia's booth conversation include at least one exchange that earns her 'full-throated laugh' through actual humor on the page, not just the narrator describing the laugh from outside. Julia's warmth per the style bible is partly comic energy — let her be funny before she's devastating.

findings.acceptance_test: Julia's total claimed timeline (proxy tenure + unassigned period) sums to no more than 38 months, fitting within the established ~3-year AI governance operational window plus the ~6 weeks elapsed by the second Thursday of April. The corrected numbers must appear at lines 37 and/or 59 and must be internally consistent with her described experience level and emotional state.
findings.evidence: Julia claims 'Three years. The whole early period' as a Llama-state proxy (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:37), then 'another eight months as a proxy' after her divorce before Llama pulled out (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:53), then 'Fourteen months' unassigned (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:59). Even reading the 8 months as included within the 3 years (most natural reading), Julia's timeline requires 36 months proxy + 14 months unassigned = 50 months minimum. The continuity sheet establishes AI governance as 'operational for roughly three years' as of late February 2029 (~36 months). Chapter 10 occurs on the second Thursday of April, adding ~6 weeks: ~38 months total available. Julia's timeline exceeds the available window by approximately 12 months.
findings.problem: Julia cannot have been a proxy for three years AND been unassigned for fourteen months within a governance system that has existed for approximately three years and two months. The math requires ~50 months of AI governance history where only ~38 months are available. This is a continuity contradiction with the timeline established in the continuity sheet.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:37: reduce Julia's proxy tenure from 'Three years' to approximately 'Two years' or 'a year and a half,' which would allow the 14-month unassigned period to fit within the established timeline. Alternatively, at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:59: reduce 'Fourteen months' to a shorter unassigned period (e.g., 'six months' or 'eight months') that fits within the timeline. Either adjustment must remain consistent with Julia's characterization as someone who was there for the 'early period' of Llama-state and has been unassigned long enough for the emptiness to have settled in. The phrase 'the whole early period' at line 37 should be retained or adapted to match the corrected tenure.

findings.acceptance_test: Julia's stated subway line is consistent with her stated neighborhood. If she lived in Sunset Park, the train is the D, N, or R. If the L train is retained, her neighborhood must be one the L serves.
findings.evidence: Julia says: 'I miss the commute. The L train, every morning, packed with other proxies' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:77). Julia lived in a 'one-bedroom in Sunset Park' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:49) during her proxy work.
findings.problem: The L train does not serve Sunset Park. The L train runs along 14th Street in Manhattan and through Williamsburg, Bushwick, East New York, and Canarsie in Brooklyn. Sunset Park is served by the D, N, and R trains along the Fourth Avenue line. A Sunset Park resident would not take the L train as a primary commuter line. This is a geographic error.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_10.md:77: replace 'The L train' with a subway line that serves Sunset Park — the D, N, or R train (all stop at 36th Street or nearby stations in Sunset Park). The N or R are the most natural choices for a Sunset Park commuter. Alternatively, if the L train detail is important for texture or specificity, change Julia's pre-divorce neighborhood from 'Sunset Park' at line 49 to a neighborhood the L train actually serves (e.g., Bushwick, Williamsburg, East New York).

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel's risk zone is the gap between dignity and absurdity. The characters are adults with professional histories, a child, a mortgage — people with legitimate self-respect — who have been reduced to warm bodies in an optimization framework they cannot understand. The comedy must not protect them from the humiliation; the humiliation must not neutralize the comedy. Render both registers at full force, often in the same scene. The temptation will be to soften the comedy into gentle satire or to darken the humiliation into dystopian grimness — resist both. The novel's territory is the painful and the funny coexisting without one defusing the other. Commit to the specific physical embarrassment of being professionally irrelevant. Commit to the specific tenderness of parenting under surveillance. Commit to the specific grief of a marriage that might still be alive inside a system that has classified it as infrastructure. When in doubt, choose the version that makes you wince and then laugh, or laugh and then wince — both are correct.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism is a craft failure equivalent to a plot hole. When a scene requires the reader to sit with discomfort — economic shame, marital cruelty, parental failure, institutional humiliation, the specific indignity of being professionally redundant — render it with full specificity. 'They struggled with the situation' is not a sentence that belongs in this novel. Name the thing. Show the body. Let the reader be uncomfortable.
aesthetic_risk_policy.intimacy_and_desire_policy: Dan and Mira's residual physical attraction is a narrative force, not background radiation. When the novel's relational pressure calls for it, render desire with the same specificity as any other emotional state — where in the body it lives, how it changes the air in a room, the precise quality of proximity when two people who know each other's bodies are trying to pretend they don't. The almost-kiss in Chapter 7 should be as fully rendered as any confrontation scene.
aesthetic_risk_policy.register_commitment: This is a dark comedy, not a cautionary tale, not a dystopia, not a satire with a thesis. The novel should not argue about AI. It should render what it feels like to be a body in a world where bodies are overhead. The comic register is not a veneer over a serious message — it IS the message. A scene that is genuinely funny about genuine suffering is doing more literary work than a scene that is solemnly observant about the same material.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the default for all spoken English in this novel. Uncontracted forms should be reserved for deliberate emphasis, Lena Morse's institutional register, or moments where a character is being carefully precise. If a line of dialogue uses 'do not' instead of 'don't' or 'I am' instead of 'I'm,' there should be a reason audible in the scene's pressure.
dialogue_rules.interruption_and_overlap_policy: Use interruptions, sentence fragments, and crosstalk when scene pressure warrants. Dan and Mira in particular should interrupt each other — they know each other's arguments before the sentences end. But every interruption must be motivated; do not scatter dashes for rhythmic texture alone.
dialogue_rules.leverage_shift_guidance: Every exchange of more than three lines should shift the power balance between speakers. This doesn't require overt conflict — a silence, a deflection, a piece of information withheld or accidentally revealed, a joke that lands wrong, a question left unanswered can all shift leverage. The dialogue must be going somewhere, not circling.
dialogue_rules.silence_as_dialogue: Silence, non-answers, and topic changes are valid dialogue moves and often more revealing than speech. When a character doesn't respond to something, the reader should feel the weight of what wasn't said. Render these moments through physical action or observed stillness, not by narrating that the character chose not to speak.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: No two consecutive chapters should share the same structural rhythm. Vary: scene count (single-scene compression vs. multi-scene chapters), dialogue density (some chapters are conversation-heavy, others are nearly silent), interiority depth (some chapters live mostly in the POV character's head, others are externally rendered), pacing (some chapters cover hours, others cover weeks in montage), and tonal register (comic chapters adjacent to devastating ones). The contrast between chapters is itself a narrative tool — use it to create whiplash, irony, and accumulating emotional force.
prose_style_profile.diction: Contemporary, precise, and register-sensitive. The prose absorbs the vocabulary of its setting: corporate language, tech terminology, bureaucratic formulas — but always filtered through the POV character's relationship to that language. Dan uses corporate words reflexively and with slight discomfort. Mira uses strategic vocabulary as native speech. The narrator should not editorialize on the language; let the words sit in context and let the reader hear the dissonance. Avoid: prestige-literary abstractions that haven't been earned by sensory grounding, and journalistic explainer-prose that breaks character to tell the reader about the world.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, rotating between Dan and Mira as primary POV characters. The camera stays tight to the current POV character's perceptions and vocabulary — what Dan notices is different from what Mira notices. In shared-POV chapters, the rotation should be clear (section breaks or sustained passages in one POV before shifting) rather than head-hopping within a scene.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied and responsive to scene pressure. Short declarative sentences for comic timing, for shock, and for moments of clarity. Longer, more syntactically complex sentences for interiority, sensory immersion, and emotional complexity. The rhythm should accelerate during confrontation and slow during reflection. Avoid settling into any single rhythmic default — if three consecutive paragraphs use the same sentence structure, one of them is wrong.
